source_paper_title: Asymptotic Optimality of Constant-Order Policies in Joint Pricing and Inventory Models
source_paper_bibtex: @article{Chen_2024, title={Asymptotic Optimality of Constant-Order Policies in Joint Pricing and Inventory Models}, volume={49}, ISSN={1526-5471}, url={http://dx.doi.org/10.1287/moor.2023.1367}, DOI={10.1287/moor.2023.1367}, number={1}, journal={Mathematics of Operations Research}, publisher={Institute for Operations Research and the Management Sciences (INFORMS)}, author={Chen, Xin and Stolyar, Alexander L. and Xin, Linwei}, year={2024}, month=feb, pages={557–577} }
context:
Page 6, Section 3.1 “Additional definitions and notations”, Remarks (bullet discussing moment assumptions).

By combining the arguments of the present paper (which assumes finite third moment but allows \(D\) to have bounded support) with the arguments of the aforementioned earlier version, we would in principle obtain asymptotic optimality assuming only a finite second moment. For simplicity of exposition, we do not formalize this generalization, and leave as an open question the minimal required assumptions on \(D\) for such an asymptotic optimality to hold.
title: Determine minimal moment assumptions for asymptotic optimality of constant-order policies
background_latex:
Consider the single-item, periodic-review, lost-sales inventory model with deterministic lead time \(L\in\mathbb{Z}_{>0}\) and planning horizon \(T\in\mathbb{Z}_{>L}\). Demands \(\{D_t\}_{t\ge 1}\) are i.i.d. copies of a nonnegative, non-deterministic random variable \(D\) with finite mean \(\mu=\mathbb{E}[D]\) and variance \(\sigma^2=\mathrm{Var}(D)\in(0,\infty)\) (and possibly higher moments). Inventory on hand at the start of period \(t\) is \(I_t\ge 0\), and the pipeline vector is \(x_t=(x_{1,t},\dots,x_{L,t})\in\mathbb{R}_+^L\), where \(x_{i,t}\) is the quantity scheduled to arrive in \(i\) periods. Each period: (1) \(x_{1,t}\) arrives and is added to inventory; (2) the controller places an order (a nonnegative quantity measurable with respect to current state and model primitives) that becomes \(x_{L,t+1}\); (3) demand \(D_t\) realizes; (4) end-of-period inventory is \(I_{t+1}=(I_t+x_{1,t}-D_t)^+\) and lost sales are \(N_t=(I_t+x_{1,t}-D_t)^-\). Costs per period are holding \(h I_{t+1}\) and lost-sales penalty \(c N_t\) with \(h,c>0\), so \(C_t=h(I_t+x_{1,t}-D_t)^+ + c(I_t+x_{1,t}-D_t)^-\). The admissible policies \(\Pi\) are all nonanticipative ordering rules using \((t,T,L,h,c,\text{law of }D,I_t,x_t)\). The performance criterion is expected total cost \(\mathbb{E}[\sum_{t=L+1}^{T+L} C_t]\) with fixed initial state \(I_0=0\) and \(x_0=0\). Let \(\mathrm{OPT}(L,T)=\inf_{\pi\in\Pi}\mathbb{E}[\sum_{t=L+1}^{T+L} C_t^{\pi}]\).

A (stationarized) constant-order policy is parameterized by \(r\in[0,\mu)\): it orders \(I_\infty^r+r\) in the first period and then orders \(r\) each subsequent period, where \(I_\infty^r\) has the steady-state waiting-time distribution of a stable \(GI/D/1\) queue with interarrival distribution \(D\) and deterministic service time \(r\), i.e. \(I_\infty^r\stackrel{d}{=}\sup_{k\ge 0}(kr-\sum_{i=1}^k D_i)\). Let \(z\in\arg\min_{v\ge 0}\big(h\,\mathbb{E}[I_\infty^v]-cv\big)\) (take the smallest minimizer if not unique), and denote by \(\pi_z\) the corresponding constant-order policy. The paper proves asymptotic optimality of \(\pi_z\) as \(L\to\infty\) under the standing assumption that \(\mathbb{E}[D^3]<\infty\) (finite third moment).
problem_definition_latex:
Find the weakest (minimal) integrability/moment assumptions on the demand distribution \(D\) (beyond nonnegativity, non-determinism, and \(\mu=\mathbb{E}[D]<\infty\)) under which the constant-order policy \(\pi_z\) is asymptotically optimal as lead time grows, in the sense that
\[
\lim_{L\to\infty}\;\limsup_{T\to\infty}\;\frac{\mathbb{E}\big[\sum_{t=L+1}^{T+L} C_t^{\pi_z}\big]}{\mathrm{OPT}(L,T)}=1.
\]
In particular, determine whether finite second moment \(\mathbb{E}[D^2]<\infty\) (or still weaker conditions) suffices in full generality, and if not, characterize a minimal condition that does.
latex:
Consider the single-item, periodic-review, lost-sales inventory model with deterministic lead time \(L\in\mathbb{Z}_{>0}\) and planning horizon \(T\in\mathbb{Z}_{>L}\). Demands \(\{D_t\}_{t\ge 1}\) are i.i.d. copies of a nonnegative, non-deterministic random variable \(D\) with finite mean \(\mu=\mathbb{E}[D]\) and variance \(\sigma^2=\mathrm{Var}(D)\in(0,\infty)\) (and possibly higher moments). Inventory on hand at the start of period \(t\) is \(I_t\ge 0\), and the pipeline vector is \(x_t=(x_{1,t},\dots,x_{L,t})\in\mathbb{R}_+^L\), where \(x_{i,t}\) is the quantity scheduled to arrive in \(i\) periods. The performance criterion is expected total cost \(\mathbb{E}[\sum_{t=L+1}^{T+L} C_t]\) with fixed initial state \(I_0=0\) and \(x_0=0\). Let \(\mathrm{OPT}(L,T)=\inf_{\pi\in\Pi}\mathbb{E}[\sum_{t=L+1}^{T+L} C_t^{\pi}]\).

A (stationarized) constant-order policy is parameterized by \(r\in[0,\mu)\): it orders \(I_\infty^r+r\) in the first period and then orders \(r\) each subsequent period, where \(I_\infty^r\) has the steady-state waiting-time distribution of a stable \(GI/D/1\) queue with interarrival distribution \(D\) and deterministic service time \(r\), i.e. \(I_\infty^r\stackrel{d}{=}\sup_{k\ge 0}(kr-\sum_{i=1}^k D_i)\). Find the weakest (minimal) integrability/moment assumptions on the demand distribution \(D\) (beyond nonnegativity, non-determinism, and \(\mu=\mathbb{E}[D]<\infty\)) under which the constant-order policy \(\pi_z\) is asymptotically optimal as lead time grows, in the sense that
\[
\lim_{L\to\infty}\;\limsup_{T\to\infty}\;\frac{\mathbb{E}\big[\sum_{t=L+1}^{T+L} C_t^{\pi_z}\big]}{\mathrm{OPT}(L,T)}=1.
\]

location_in_paper: Page 6, Section 3.1 “Additional definitions and notations”, Remarks (bullet discussing moment assumptions).
open_problem_quote: By combining the arguments of the present paper (which assumes finite third moment but allows \(D\) to have bounded support) with the arguments of the aforementioned earlier version, we would in principle obtain asymptotic optimality assuming only a finite second moment. For simplicity of exposition, we do not formalize this generalization, and leave as an open question the minimal required assumptions on \(D\) for such an asymptotic optimality to hold.
counterexample_suitability_rationale: The question asks for weakest distributional assumptions guaranteeing a universal asymptotic statement over all horizons and lead times, which is hard to refute by finite instance search. Automated search could suggest heavy-tailed distributions where performance degrades, but establishing failure would likely require asymptotic/probabilistic analysis rather than a finite counterexample.
alphaevolve_suitability_rationale: One can programmatically evaluate the constant-order policy and approximate OPT for small lead times/horizons via dynamic programming on discretized demand distributions to explore which tail behaviors appear problematic. However, the fitness signal is only a proxy for the true asymptotic regime and may be computationally expensive/noisy when approximating long lead times or near-critical \(r\uparrow\mathbb{E}[D]\).

verification.notice: Generated from the paper text only and not independently verified as still open.

literature_review.synthesis:
The original paper (Goldberg–Katz-Rogozhnikov–Lu–Sharma–Squillante, arXiv:1211.4063) proves that the stationarized constant-order policy \(\pi_z\), defined via the steady-state waiting time \(I_\infty^r\stackrel{d}{=}\sup_{k\ge 0}(kr-\sum_{i=1}^k D_i)\) of a stable \(GI/D/1\) queue, is asymptotically optimal as lead time \(L\to\infty\) for the finite-horizon lost-sales objective \(\mathbb{E}[\sum_{t=L+1}^{T+L} C_t]\). The proof’s only explicit higher-moment requirement is \(\mathbb{E}[D^3]<\infty\), used through a Stein/Berry–Esseen-type quantitative CLT (their Theorem 11) inside Lemma 10 to show the “mimicking” average pipeline rate \(r^*\) is bounded away from \(\mu=\mathbb{E}[D]\). The authors explicitly remark that this third-moment assumption is not intrinsic and that an earlier version obtained similar results under \(\mathbb{E}[D^2]<\infty\) but with an unbounded-support condition; they leave open the minimal integrability assumptions needed in full generality.

Subsequent work has not (based on the provided forward-citation set) closed this gap. The closest partial progress continues to rely on \(\mathbb{E}[D^3]<\infty\) for the same quantitative-CLT step, while explicitly suggesting that a hybrid of proof techniques should extend the asymptotic optimality of \(\pi_z\) to the finite-variance case \(\mathbb{E}[D^2]<\infty\). Complementary advances analyze constant-order policies under only second moments using Wiener–Hopf and ladder-process methods, yielding explicit structure for relative value functions and integrability bounds that align with the conjecture that finite variance is the “right” threshold. Other related papers obtain stronger rates (even exponential in \(L\)) but under light-tail/exponential-moment assumptions and/or different objectives (infinite-horizon average cost), and general MDP large-lead-time results that may offer transferable tools but do not directly address the moment-minimality question.

Overall, the minimal-moment problem remains open: existing proofs of the finite-horizon \(\lim_{L\to\infty}\limsup_{T\to\infty}\) ratio for \(\pi_z\) either assume \(\mathbb{E}[D^3]<\infty\) or add distributional restrictions (e.g., unbounded support or exponential moments). A promising direction is to replace the Stein-rate CLT step used to control \(\mu-r^*\) with arguments that require only \(\mathbb{E}[D^2]<\infty\), e.g., truncation plus Lindeberg-type CLTs for the positive-part functional \(x\mapsto (x)^+\), or renewal/ladder-height estimates for \(\sup_{k\ge 0}(kr-\sum_{i=1}^k D_i)\) that yield uniform-in-\(L\) lower bounds on holding costs when \(r\uparrow \mu\) without needing third moments.

sources.title: UCB-type learning algorithms for lost-sales inventory models with lead times
sources.theorem_or_result:
The paper proves asymptotic optimality of a stationarized constant-order policy \(\pi_z\) for the periodic-review lost-sales model with deterministic lead time \(L\) and i.i.d. demand \(D\), under the assumption \(\mathbb{E}[D^3]<\infty\): for all \(\varepsilon\in(0,1)\) and sufficiently large \(L\) (explicitly, \(L\ge y(\varepsilon)\)) and \(T\ge (1+3\varepsilon^{-1})L\),
\[
\frac{\mathbb{E}[\sum_{t=L+1}^{T+L} C_t^{\pi_z}]}{\mathrm{OPT}(L,T)}\le 1+\varepsilon,
\]
so \(\lim_{L\to\infty}\limsup_{T\to\infty} \mathbb{E}[\sum_{t=L+1}^{T+L} C_t^{\pi_z}]/\mathrm{OPT}(L,T)=1\) (Theorem 1 and Corollary 2). The authors also state that by combining this version’s arguments (finite third moment, bounded support allowed) with an earlier version’s arguments (finite second moment, unbounded support required), one could in principle obtain asymptotic optimality under \(\mathbb{E}[D^2]<\infty\), but they do not formalize it and leave the minimal assumptions open.
sources.connection: The open problem asks for the weakest moment/integrability assumptions on \(D\) ensuring asymptotic optimality of \(\pi_z\) as \(L\to\infty\), and in particular whether \(\mathbb{E}[D^2]<\infty\) suffices. This citing paper is essentially the main source establishing asymptotic optimality, but it assumes \(\mathbb{E}[D^3]<\infty\) to use a Berry–Esseen/Stein-type CLT rate (Theorem 11) in Lemma 10 to bound \(\mathbb{E}[D]-r^*\) away from 0. Crucially, the authors explicitly remark that finite third moment is ‘not strictly necessary’; they reference an earlier version achieving similar results under finite second moment but with an additional unbounded-support condition, and they state that combining the two approaches should yield asymptotic optimality under finite second moment in general, yet they leave this as an open question. Thus the paper provides partial progress and identifies where the third moment enters, but it does not resolve the minimal-moment question.

sources.title: Asymptotic Optimality of Simple Heuristic Policies for Perishable and Lost-Sales Inventory Systems
sources.theorem_or_result:
Main technical results (Moradi–Arts–Drent, 2025):
- Under i.i.d. nonnegative demand with finite mean and finite variance \(\mathbb E[D^2]<\infty\), they solve the Wiener–Hopf equation for the relative value function \(v_r\) of the constant-order policy \(C_r\) and show
\[v_r(x)=\frac{h}{2(\mu_D-r)}\Big((x-\tilde\xi(r))^2-\tilde\xi(r)^2+2\mu_+\int_0^x g_r(y)\,dy\Big),\qquad x\ge 0,\]
where \(\mu_+=\mathbb E[S_{\tau_+}]\) is the mean ascending ladder height of the random walk with increments \(Y=D-r\), \(U_+\) is the ascending ladder renewal measure with expansion \(U_+(x)=x/\mu_+ + \kappa + g_r(x)\), and \(g_r(x)=o(1)\).
- They prove a uniform (in \(p\)) bounded-gap comparison between the best projected-inventory-level (PIL) policy and the best constant-order policy as \(L\to\infty\): there exists \(M<\infty\) such that
\[\lim_{L\to\infty}\big(C(P_{\xi_p}\mid p,L)-C^*(p,L)\big)\le M,\]
and also a two-parameter asymptotic optimality statement in the regime \(p\to\infty\) after \(L\to\infty\):
\[\lim_{p\to\infty}\lim_{L\to\infty}\frac{C(P_{\xi_p}\mid p,L)}{C^*(p,L)}=1.\]
sources.connection:
The open problem asks for minimal moment assumptions on demand \(D\) under which the constant-order policy \(\pi_z\) is asymptotically optimal as \(L\to\infty\) (in a finite-horizon formulation) and in particular whether \(\mathbb E[D^2]<\infty\) suffices.

This citing paper does not re-prove constant-order asymptotic optimality under weaker moments; instead it *assumes* the known asymptotic optimality of constant-order policies from Goldberg et al. (2016) / Xin–Goldberg (2016) to transfer asymptotic statements to PIL policies. However, it develops new Wiener–Hopf/ladder-process analysis of the constant-order policy’s relative value function under only \(\mathbb E[D^2]<\infty\), yielding a quadratic-plus-sublinear representation and integrability bounds (e.g., finiteness of \(\mathbb E[v_r(Z)]\) for \(Z\) with finite second moment).

Thus, relative to the open problem, it provides partial progress/strong evidence that second-moment assumptions are natural and sufficient for key analytic ingredients around constant-order policies (value-function structure, heavy-traffic scaling), but it does not establish the full asymptotic optimality ratio \(\to 1\) for the constant-order policy itself under \(\mathbb E[D^2]<\infty\), nor does it address the exact finite-horizon double limit \(\lim_{L\to\infty}\limsup_{T\to\infty}\) used in the open problem.

sources.title: Expected Profit of the Best Fixed-Price Policy Could Decay Exponentially in Lead Time
sources.theorem_or_result:
For the infinite-horizon lost-sales model with i.i.d. nonnegative demand with finite mean and positive variance, deterministic lead time \(L\), holding cost \(h\) and lost-sales penalty \(p\), let \(\pi_{r_\infty}\) be the best constant-order policy where \(r_\infty\in\arg\min_{0\le r<\mathbb E[D]}\{h\,\mathbb E[I_\infty^r]+p\mathbb E[D]-pr\}\) and \(I_\infty^r\stackrel d=\sup_{j\ge0}(jr-\sum_{i=1}^j D_i)\). Define \(\phi(\theta)=e^{\theta r_\infty}\mathbb E[e^{-\theta D}]\), \(\gamma=\inf_{\theta\ge0}\phi(\theta)\in[0,1)\), and let \(g=\inf_{x\in\mathbb R}\mathbb E[h(x-D)^+ + p(x-D)^-]>0\). Then for all \(L\ge1\),
\[
\frac{C(\pi_{r_\infty})}{\mathrm{OPT}(L)}\le 1+\frac{h}{(1-\gamma)g}\Big(\mathbb E[D]-r_\infty+\frac{1}{e\vartheta(L+1)}\Big)\gamma^{L+1},
\]
so the optimality gap of \(\pi_{r_\infty}\) decays exponentially in \(L\) for the infinite-horizon average-cost objective.
sources.connection:
The open problem asks for minimal moment/integrability assumptions ensuring asymptotic optimality of the (stationarized) constant-order policy \(\pi_z\) for the finite-horizon objective as \(L\to\infty\), and in particular whether a finite second moment suffices.

This paper strengthens the *rate* of convergence to optimality (exponential in \(L\)) but only for the *infinite-horizon average-cost* formulation \(\mathrm{OPT}(L)=\inf_{\pi} \limsup_{T\to\infty} \frac1T\sum_{t=1}^T\mathbb E[C_t^\pi]\). It assumes only \(\mathbb E[D]<\infty\) and \(\mathrm{Var}(D)>0\) at the model level, but its exponential bound uses a Chernoff/large-deviations parameter \(\gamma=\inf_{\theta\ge0} e^{\theta r_\infty}\mathbb E[e^{-\theta D}]\), which is strictly less than 1 only when the Laplace transform \(\mathbb E[e^{-\theta D}]\) is finite for some \(\theta>0\) (i.e. a light-tail/exponential-moment condition). Thus it does not resolve the finite-horizon minimal-moment question; rather, it provides related progress under (implicitly) stronger tail assumptions and in a different horizon regime.

sources.title: Asymptotic optimality of semi-open-loop policies in markov decision processes with large lead times
sources.theorem_or_result: From the title/abstract only: the paper establishes asymptotic optimality (as lead time \,\(L\to\infty\)) of a class of “semi-open-loop” policies for a generic Markov decision process with one immediate control and one delayed control. The result appears to be a general theorem showing that restricting to semi-open-loop policies incurs vanishing optimality gap as \(L\to\infty\) under the paper’s MDP assumptions (not provided in the abstract).
sources.connection: The open problem asks for minimal moment/integrability assumptions on i.i.d. demand \(D\) under which a specific constant-order policy \(\pi_z\) is asymptotically optimal in the lost-sales inventory model as \(L\to\infty\). This citing paper is thematically related (asymptotic optimality with large lead times) and may provide a general MDP framework/techniques that could potentially be specialized to inventory models. However, based on the title/abstract alone, it does not address (i) constant-order policies specifically, (ii) the GI/D/1 steady-state construction \(I_\infty^r\), or (iii) the minimal moment assumptions on \(D\) (e.g., whether \(\mathbb{E}[D^2]<\infty\) suffices). Without the full text, there is no evidence it resolves or even directly advances the moment-assumption question.

sources.title: Lead Time Independent Joint Inventory and Pricing Control for Lost Sales System with Poisson Demand
sources.theorem_or_result: From the title/abstract only: the paper proposes a joint inventory-and-pricing control policy for a lost-sales system with Poisson demand whose performance (or structure) is independent of the lead time, i.e. a “lead-time independent” control that is presumably near-optimal or optimal within that Poisson-demand setting.
sources.connection: The open problem asks for minimal moment/integrability assumptions on a general i.i.d. demand distribution under which a constant-order policy is asymptotically optimal as lead time \(L\to\infty\). This citing paper appears to study a different but related setting: joint inventory and pricing control under lost sales with Poisson demand (a specific light-tailed distribution with all moments finite). If the paper proves asymptotic optimality (or a constant-order/constant-base-stock type structure) with guarantees uniform in \(L\), that would provide partial progress only for the special case of Poisson demand and possibly with pricing decisions included. However, based on title/abstract alone, there is no evidence it addresses minimal moment assumptions or extends results to heavy-tailed/low-moment demands.

sources.title: Constant-order policies for lost-sales inventory models with random supply functions: Asymptotics and heuristic
sources.theorem_or_result: From the title/abstract only: the paper studies constant-order policies in an infinite-horizon lost-sales inventory model with positive lead times where the supply received is a random function of the order quantity (e.g., random yield/capacity). It derives asymptotic performance results and proposes/justifies a constant-order heuristic for this random-supply setting.
sources.connection: The open problem asks for minimal moment (integrability) assumptions on i.i.d. demand under which constant-order policies are asymptotically optimal as deterministic lead time \(L\to\infty\) in the standard lost-sales model with deterministic supply. The citing paper appears to extend the constant-order-policy paradigm to a different model class (random supply function/yield) and proves asymptotic results there. Based on the abstract alone, it does not address weakening the demand moment assumptions (e.g., from \(\mathbb{E}[D^3]<\infty\) to \(\mathbb{E}[D^2]<\infty\) or less) in the deterministic-supply model, nor does it characterize minimal integrability conditions. It may contain techniques (e.g., coupling/renewal/queueing arguments) that could be adapted, but this cannot be confirmed without the full text.
